sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Creation of an Executable by an Executable
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description: Detects the creation of an executable by another executable

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert ip any any -> any any ( msg:"POLICY-OTHER IGMP membership query attempt"; ip_proto:2; content:"|11|",depth 1; reference:cve,2019-12259; reference:cve,2019-12265; classtype:protocol-command-decode; sid:51037; rev:1; )

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This binary acts as a targeted Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) HTTP proxy. It initializes a listener on TCP port 8080 (0x1f90) and spawns handler threads for intercepted connections. The sample performs manual HTTP header parsing (GET, POST, Host, and Cookie fields) and executes conditional traffic redirection. Specifically, it monitors for traffic related to 'today.hit.edu.cn' and the internal IP '172.20.56.45', with logic to rewrite requested URLs (e.g., redirecting 'category/10' to 'category/11'). This behavior is consistent with a traffic-interception trojan used for session hijacking or content manipulation.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This binary functions as a specialized HTTP proxy Trojan or Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) tool. It sets up a local network listener on port 8080 (0x1f90) using WSAStartup, socket, bind, and listen. Upon accepting a connection, it spawns a thread (sub_416420) that parses incoming HTTP traffic (GET/POST requests and Cookies) via sub_411d90. The malware specifically monitors and potentially redirects traffic destined for the Harbin Institute of Technology news portal (today.hit.edu.cn) and interacts with a hardcoded internal IP address (172.20.56.45). The behavior of intercepting, parsing, and conditionally manipulating web traffic to a specific educational domain is characteristic of a targeted redirection Trojan.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This binary functions as a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) or transparent proxy. It initializes a listening socket on port 8080 (0x1f90) and spawns threads to handle concurrent connections. The core malicious logic (sub_416420) intercepts network traffic, identifies specific triggers like 'http://csszengarden.com/', and programmatically replaces them with 'http://today.hit.edu.cn/category/11'. It effectively hijacks and redirects web requests to a target host (today.hit.edu.cn). The sample also contains unusual logic for locating and loading PDB-related DLLs (MSPDB140.DLL) via registry path lookups for Visual Studio, which may be used for advanced debugging, environment fingerprinting, or as an anti-analysis mechanism. Key IOCs include IP 172.20.56.45, port 8080, and the redirection target today.hit.edu.cn.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This sample functions as a Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) HTTP proxy and traffic interceptor. It initializes a listening socket on port 8080 (sub_411b70) and uses a multi-threaded approach (sub_412620) to process incoming connections. The core malicious logic (sub_416420) involves parsing HTTP requests, specifically identifying traffic directed to 'today.hit.edu.cn'. It then performs string-based hijacking to redirect or modify the destination to 'www.baidu.com' using memcpy and strncpy before forwarding the modified packets. Relevant IOCs include the IP 172.20.56.45, port 8080, and targeted hijacking of the HIT educational domain.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This sample functions as a targeted network interception tool or Trojan. It initializes a listener on TCP port 8080 (0x1f90) and spawns handler threads for incoming connections. The core logic (sub_416420) performs manual HTTP header parsing, specifically inspecting traffic for the domain 'today.hit.edu.cn'. It exhibits capabilities for URL rewriting and redirection, notably swapping request paths between '/category/10' and '/category/11'. It includes hardcoded references to an internal IP address (172.20.56.45) and uses standard WinSock APIs (bind, listen, accept, connect, send, recv) to proxy and modify traffic between a victim and the target server. Relevant IOCs include the targeted domain, the hardcoded internal IP, and the use of port 8080 for interception.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This sample is a network backdoor and traffic redirection tool (reverse proxy). It establishes a TCP server listening on port 8080 (0x1F90) and spawns a new thread for every incoming connection. The core logic in sub_416420 functions as a Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) relay: it captures incoming network buffers, parses and modifies HTTP headers (including Cookies and Host fields via sub_411d90), and performs traffic redirection to external targets like 'www.baidu.com' or hardcoded URLs related to 'today.hit.edu.cn'. It specifically filters traffic from the internal IP 172.20.56.45. This functionality is consistent with specialized malware used for exfiltration, traffic interception, or lateral movement within a targeted network infrastructure.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This binary functions as a malicious intercepting proxy or traffic redirector. It establishes a local TCP listener on port 8080 (0x1f90) via sub_411b70 and sub_412620. Upon accepting a connection, it spawns a thread (sub_416420) that captures incoming HTTP traffic. The sample contains specific logic to parse HTTP headers (sub_411d90), identifying 'GET', 'POST', 'Host', and 'Cookie' fields. Most notably, sub_416420 contains hardcoded logic to redirect traffic intended for 'http://csszengarden.com/' to 'http://today.hit.edu.cn/category/11/'. It also references a specific internal IP (172.20.56.45) and uses standard socket APIs (connect, send, recv) to facilitate Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) behavior.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This PE binary functions as a malicious HTTP proxy and traffic interceptor. It initializes a local server on port 8080 (0x1f90) using WinSock (sub_411b70), accepts incoming connections, and spawns worker threads (sub_412620). The thread handler (sub_416420) performs Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) operations: it receives data from the client, parses HTTP headers (GET, POST, and Cookies) via sub_411d90, and implements hardcoded redirection logic. Specifically, it monitors for traffic destined for 'today.hit.edu.cn' or the internal IP '172.20.56.45', and substitutes the request destination with 'www.baidu.com'. This behavior is characteristic of redirection trojans or data interception tools. Relevant IOCs include port 8080, IP 172.20.56.45, and the domain today.hit.edu.cn.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: The binary acts as a multi-threaded TCP redirection proxy with Man-in-the-Middle (MitM) capabilities. It initializes a listener on port 8080 (0x1f90) and, for each accepted connection, spawns a worker thread (sub_416420) that connects to a remote host on port 80. The sample specifically monitors traffic for an internal IP (172.20.56.45) and manipulates HTTP-like requests, specifically targeting the domain 'today.hit.edu.cn' and redirecting or replacing data with 'csszengarden.com'. Such behavior is indicative of a tool used for traffic interception, unauthorized redirection, or bypassing network filters. Additionally, it contains unusual logic to locate and load Visual Studio debugging components (MSPDB140.DLL) via registry path lookups.

attributes.magic: PE32 executable (console) Intel 80386, for MS Windows

crowdsourced_ai_results.analysis: This binary functions as a man-in-the-middle (MITM) proxy server that listens on port 8080 (0x1f90). The sample utilizes Windows Sockets (Winsock) to accept incoming connections and spawns threads (sub_416420) to intercept and modify HTTP traffic. Specifically, it parses HTTP headers (Host, Cookie) and contains hardcoded logic to redirect requests originally destined for 'csszengarden.com' to 'today.hit.edu.cn'. Such behavior is characteristic of traffic injectors or credential-harvesting proxies. Relevant IOCs include the IP address 172.20.56.45 and the target domains hit.edu.cn and csszengarden.com.

trid.file_type: Excel Microsoft Office Open XML Format document

sigma_analysis_results.rule_title: Suspicious New Instance Of An Office COM Object
sigma_analysis_results.rule_description:
Detects an svchost process spawning an instance of an office application. This happens when the initial word application creates an instance of one of the Office COM objects such as 'Word.Application', 'Excel.Application', etc.
This can be used by malicious actors to create malicious Office documents with macros on the fly. (See vba2clr project in the references)

trid.file_type: Excel Microsoft Office Open XML Format document
Word Microsoft Office Open XML Format document

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_msg: ET INFO Observed Google DNS over HTTPS Domain (dns .google in TLS SNI)

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an Adobe Type 1 Font. The Type 1 Font Format is a standardized font format for digital imaging applications.
crowdsourced_yara_results.author: InQuest Labs

crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_msg: ET INFO Observed Google DNS over HTTPS Domain (dns .google in TLS SNI)
crowdsourced_ids_results.rule_raw: alert tls $HOME_NET any -> $EXTERNAL_NET any (msg:"ET INFO Observed Google DNS over HTTPS Domain (dns .google in TLS SNI)"; flow:established,to_server; threshold: type both, track by_src, count 1, seconds 600; tls.sni; dotprefix; content:".dns.google"; endswith; reference:url,developers.google.com/speed/public-dns/docs/doh/; classtype:misc-activity; sid:2047866; rev:4; metadata:affected_product Any, attack_target Client_Endpoint, created_at 2022_02_07, deployment Perimeter, performance_impact Low, confidence High, signature_severity Informational, tag DoH, updated_at 2023_10_05, reviewed_at 2023_10_05, former_sid 2851058; target:src_ip;)

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature detects an Adobe Type 1 Font. The Type 1 Font Format is a standardized font format for digital imaging applications.

crowdsourced_yara_results.description: This signature identifies Adobe Extensible Metadata Platform (XMP) identifiers embedded within files. Defined as a standard for mapping graphical asset relationships, XMP allows for tracking of both parent-child relationships and individual revisions. There are three categories of identifiers: original document, document, and instance. Generally, XMP data is stored in XML format, updated on save/copy, and embedded within the graphical asset. These identifiers can be used to track both malicious and benign graphics within common Microsoft and Adobe document lures.
